mnemonics.title: Remember Integrity Rules
mnemonics.content: E.R.P. - Entity integrity, Referential integrity, Primary key - Entity ensures PK not NULL, Referential ensures FK matches PK

mnemonics.title: Key Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: S.C.P.U.F - Superkey → Candidate key → Primary key → Unique key → Foreign key - Remember: Super sets Candidate, Candidate becomes Primary, others are Unique or Foreign

mnemonics.title: Constraint Types
mnemonics.content: D.E.R.K.T - Domain, Entity, Referential, Key, Tuple - All constraints work 'together' to maintain integrity

acronyms.usage: Used to uniquely identify tuples in a relation. Cannot be NULL.
Establishes relationship between tables and enforces referential integrity.
Minimal superkey - can become primary key if selected.
Set of attributes that uniquely identifies tuples. Can have extra attributes.
Represents missing or unknown information, not zero or blank.
Commands like CREATE, ALTER, DROP to define schema.
Commands like INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE to manipulate data.

visualTips.visualization: Think of a library book - each book has a unique ISBN (primary key). No two books share the same ISBN, and you cannot have a book without an ISBN.
Think of a student ID card linked to the student's record - the card (foreign key) only works if a valid student record (primary key) exists.
Entity integrity: You must have a valid name tag (PK). Referential integrity: Your name tag must match someone in the company directory (FK→PK).
NULL is like an empty envelope - we don't know what's inside or if anything was ever put there. It's neither '0 letters' nor 'no envelope'.

analogies.analogy: Superkey is like having a full address (street, city, state, zip), while Candidate key is the minimal version - just the zip code that uniquely identifies the area. The zip code is minimal but still unique.
Like a library book having a valid member card number - you can't check out a book with a membership number that doesn't exist in the system.
Like a toll booth that only accepts specific vehicles - if you try to pass a boat through, it's rejected because it's outside the allowed domain.
Like fingerprint identification - no two people can have the same fingerprint (uniqueness), and you must have a fingerprint to be identified (minimality).
